architectureSummary.overview: The Offline Invoice system uses a local SQLite database as the primary store when offline, and syncs to a central SQL Server when connectivity is available. A .NET 6 API serves online clients, while a Worker Service handles the sync queue.
architectureSummary.requestLifecycle: ONLINE: Client → API → SQL Server. OFFLINE: Client → Local SQLite → queued for sync. SYNC: Worker polls sync queue → applies to SQL Server → marks items synced → propagates server changes back to local DB

questions.questionText: How did you implement the offline-first data sync in the invoice system?
questions.answer:
The sync system uses an event queue approach — every create/update/delete in offline mode writes a SyncEvent record to the local SQLite DB. When connectivity is restored, a background worker processes the queue:

public class SyncEvent
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string EntityType { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // "Invoice", "Client"
    public int EntityId { get; set; }
    public string Operation { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // "Create", "Update", "Delete"
    public string Payload { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // JSON of the entity state
    public DateTime CreatedAt { get; set; } = DateTime.UtcNow;
    public bool IsSynced { get; set; } = false;
    public DateTime? SyncedAt { get; set; }
    public string? SyncError { get; set; }
}

// Sync worker
public class SyncWorker : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        while (!ct.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            if (await IsConnectedAsync())
                await ProcessPendingEventsAsync(ct);

            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30), ct);
        }
    }

    private async Task ProcessPendingEventsAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var pending = await _localDb.SyncEvents
            .Where(e => !e.IsSynced)
            .OrderBy(e => e.CreatedAt)  // process in order
            .ToListAsync(ct);

        foreach (var evt in pending)
        {
            try
            {
                await ApplyToServerAsync(evt, ct);
                evt.IsSynced = true;
                evt.SyncedAt = DateTime.UtcNow;
            }
            catch (Exception ex)
            {
                evt.SyncError = ex.Message;
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Sync failed for event {Id}", evt.Id);
            }
        }
        await _localDb.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    }
}
followUpQuestions: How do you handle conflict when the same record is edited offline and online simultaneously?
How do you handle ordering of sync events?
questions.importantForInterview: Conflict resolution strategy is the hard part of offline sync. Last-write-wins is simplest. Operational transform (like Google Docs) is complex. For invoice systems, business rules often dictate resolution (e.g., a payment received server-side always wins over a status change made offline).

questions.questionText: Why did you choose Dapper over EF Core for the Offline Invoice project?
questions.answer:
The Offline Invoice project had several complex multi-table reporting queries that EF Core generated poorly. Dapper gave me full control over SQL while still mapping results to C# objects.

Complex query that Dapper handled well:

// Dapper: full SQL control for complex report
public async Task<IReadOnlyList<InvoiceSummaryDto>> GetSummaryAsync(
    DateRange range, CancellationToken ct)
{
    const string sql = @"
        SELECT
            c.Name AS ClientName,
            COUNT(i.Id) AS InvoiceCount,
            SUM(i.Amount) AS TotalAmount,
            SUM(CASE WHEN i.Status = 'Paid' THEN i.Amount ELSE 0 END) AS PaidAmount,
            MAX(i.DueDate) AS LatestDue
        FROM Clients c
        LEFT JOIN Invoices i ON i.ClientId = c.Id
            AND i.InvoiceDate BETWEEN @Start AND @End
        WHERE c.IsActive = 1
        GROUP BY c.Id, c.Name
        HAVING COUNT(i.Id) > 0
        ORDER BY TotalAmount DESC";

    var connection = _db.Database.GetDbConnection();
    var results = await connection.QueryAsync<InvoiceSummaryDto>(
        new CommandDefinition(sql,
            new { Start = range.Start, End = range.End },
            cancellationToken: ct));
    return results.ToList().AsReadOnly();
}

I used both EF Core and Dapper in the same project: EF Core for standard CRUD (adding invoices, updating clients), Dapper for the reporting queries. Same DbContext.Database.GetDbConnection() to reuse the EF Core connection string and connection pooling.
followUpQuestions: What is the N+1 problem and did you encounter it?
How does Dapper handle NULL mapping?
questions.importantForInterview: Using both EF Core and Dapper in the same project is perfectly valid and common in production. EF Core for change-tracking CRUD, Dapper for read-optimized complex queries.
